message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  The COVID-19 pandemic has hit different parts of the world differently: some regions are still in the rise of the first wave, other regions are now facing a decline after a first wave, and yet other regions have started to see a second wave. The current immunity level
                  <jats:italic>î</jats:italic>
                  in a region is closely related to the cumulative fraction infected, which primarily depends on two factors: a) the initial potential for COVID-19 in the region (often quantified by the basic reproduction number
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  ), and b) the timing, amount and effectiveness of preventive measures put in place. By means of a mathematical model including heterogeneities owing to age, social activity and susceptibility, and allowing for time-varying preventive measures, the risk for a new epidemic wave and its doubling time, and how they depend on
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  ,
                  <jats:italic>î</jats:italic>
                  and the overall effect of the current preventive measures, are investigated. Focus lies on quantifying the minimal overall effect of preventive measures
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>Min</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  needed to prevent a future outbreak. The first result shows that the current immunity level
                  <jats:italic>î</jats:italic>
                  plays a more influential roll than when immunity is obtained from vaccination. Secondly, by comparing regions with different
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  and
                  <jats:italic>î</jats:italic>
                  it is shown that regions with lower
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  and low
                  <jats:italic>î</jats:italic>
                  may now need higher preventive measures (
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>Min</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  ) compared with other regions having higher
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  but also higher
                  <jats:italic>î</jats:italic>
                  , even when such immunity levels are far from herd immunity.
                </jats:p>
title: The risk for a new COVID-19 wave – and how it depends on R 0 , the current immunity level and current restrictions
